original_abstract: This thematic issue explores the diversification and institutionalization of gender equality reforms within parliaments globally. By synthesizing perspectives from both the Global North and South, the collection move beyond traditional debates on descriptive representation to examine the complexities of building gender-sensitive institutions. Utilizing the framework of feminist institutionalism, the contributing authors investigate how internal processes, workplace cultures, and policy outputs are being reshaped to prioritize gender equality. The articles highlight the pivotal role of critical actors—including "feminist academic critical actors"—and supportive coalitions in driving change, while also addressing how local political and cultural contexts can both facilitate and hinder progress. Specific case studies illuminate various challenges, ranging from the impact of autocratic "genderwashing" and the rise of the radical right to the practical difficulties of balancing parliamentary duties with family life. By introducing methodological and conceptual innovations, such as the "most significant change" approach and the study of parliamentary groups as gendered workplaces, this issue offers a comprehensive analysis of the opportunities and barriers to transforming masculinized political spaces into inclusive environments.
humanized_abstract: The purpose of this thematic issue is to map the diversity and institutionalisation of gender equality reforms within parliaments across the world. By bringing together a collection of articles based on case studies from both the Global North and South, the issue moves beyond the traditional discussions around the challenges and virtues of descriptive gender representation to focus on the challenges and opportunities related to building gender-responsive parliaments and institutions. Through a feminist institutionalist lens, the articles look into the dynamics and changes within parliamentary structures, processes, norms and policy outcomes and examine the ways in which parliaments are being re-constituted so as to address gender equality issues. It explores the roles and actions of critical masculinities and femininities, as well as the presence of coalitions to further the goals of gender equality, looking into the ways in which different local contexts of politics and cultures affect gender equality goals and how different factors influence the different levels of resistance to women's parliamentary empowerment (beyond the straightforward autocratic 'genderwashing' and the rise of the radical right, and into more everyday, familial, balance of care/work life issues). The issue also offers an innovative take on research methodologies and new theoretical frameworks, where some of the articles use the 'most significant change' methodologies, while others foreground the dynamics of gender within parliamentary groups and look into the parliamentary workplace as a gendered institution.
